source_name: Code4rena Ethena Labs Findings and Analysis Report (2024-11)
finding_title: Blacklisted user can burn tokens during WHITELIST_ENABLED state
summary: Blacklisted user can burn tokens during WHITELIST_ENABLED state. This breaks the main invariant from the README. This could become an issue when the admin tries to redistribute the blacklisted user’s UStb balance using redistributeLockedAmount() but the blacklisted user frontruns it with a burn.
root_cause: whitelist-mode burn path checks WHITELISTED_ROLE but not BLACKLISTED_ROLE, allowing dual-role blacklisted users to burn before redistribution
broken_invariants: a blacklisted account must not be able to dispose of locked UStb by burning while blacklist enforcement is active
whitelist mode must not override blacklist restrictions when an address accidentally or operationally holds both roles
redistributeLockedAmount must not be front-runnable by the blacklisted holder through an allowed burn path
attack_preconditions: the affected code version and configuration match the public report
the report-described function sequence or state condition is reachable
attacker_capability: external user or protocol participant able to trigger the affected flow
trigger_actions: observe accounting, access-control, or availability divergence

impact.description: Blacklisted user can burn tokens during WHITELIST_ENABLED state. This breaks the main invariant from the README. This could become an issue when the admin tries to redistribute the blacklisted user’s UStb balance using redistributeLockedAmount() but the blacklisted user frontruns it with a burn.

why_not_higher: the final report classified this as Medium; impact or likelihood is bounded relative to High severity
why_not_lower: included as a validated High/Medium C4 finding with concrete affected code paths and impact narrative
common_false_positive_angles: verify reachability under current roles, pause state, and integration configuration
check whether audited commit and line links match the code under review
check whether sponsor-side operational assumptions fully remove the exploit path
mitigations: Add the conditions !hasRole(BLACKLISTED_ROLE, msg.sender) and !hasRole(BLACKLISTED_ROLE, from) to the check.
